title: The Macro Minute:  Will the Trump administration’s policies trigger a balance of payments crisis?

Howdy team 42. It's your skimper here at
Darius Dell to present our macro minute
for Monday, April 21st, 2025. Hope
everyone had a lovely Easter weekend
with the respective families. So, as
always, we'll start with the our
executive summary from today's Zoff
morning note. If you would like to
review the analysis supporting these
conclusions and to see if you need to
make any changes in your portfolio to
remain on the right side of market risk,
you need to be a client of 42 macro. So,
today's key macro question is a very big
question. we got to ask and try to
answer it is uh will the Trump
administration's policy blunders trigger
a balance of payments crisis in the US?
Our answers are US stocks are slumping
as President Trump escalated attacks on
Fed Chair Pal with the National Economic
Council Director Kevin Hasset recently
confirming that Trump is exploring his
ability to fire Jalow. This fire follows
Trump's latest criticism of the Fed
chair for not cutting rates quickly
enough. Reminder, J. Pow is not cutting
rates because easing right now would
exacerbate the sell-offs in the US
dollar and US Treasury bond market for
all the capital account and
crosscurrency basis dynamics detailed in
our April 12th and April 19th round
webcast. As such, US assets are
egregiously overpriced for any scenario
that features currency instability that
stems from a lack of central bank
independence and a disregard for the
rule of law that helps make the US
exceptional. To begin with, the US's $24
trillion net international investment
deficit is at risk of destabilizing
capital outflows if we become the United
States of Turkey. All told, the risk to
US asset valuations from a change in the
international perception of US political
risk cannot be overstated. We know that
sounds like a political statement, but
it is not. Very few firms across global
Wall Street have performed as much
extensive datadriven analysis on this
subject as we have over the past few
years. VR investing during a forturning
regime presentation. This is why we feel
confident in our research and reputation
as datadriven centrists to provide yet
another public service announcement.
Transition to our 42 micro dashboard. Uh
we got a couple of questions and a
comment. Uh so I just want to uh you
know kind of these are really important
questions and especially in the context
of our systematic signals. So I want to
make sure um you know our client base is
seeing this and hearing me address them
as well as you guys who folks outside of
our pay wall because I think these are
really big important conversations. So
this one says uh starts with cash
diversification for destabilizing US
dollar. Says for KISS followers using
USFR which are floating rate treasuries
or other US dollar denominated cash
accounts. Should we consider
diversifying into other countries
considering the potential uh risk of a
US dollar destabilizing? If so, which
one would you recommend or gold or
bitcoin the best hedges? So the the
question already answer has the answer
contained in we believe that gold and
bitcoin are the best hedges for a
destabilizing move in the US dollar that
ultimately forces the fed to implement
some version of yield curve control. You
know you could have you know high rates
on the short end to protect the dollar
from capital outflows but you could have
yield curve control to cap u bond yields
on the long end. In my opinion, I think
that's where we're headed if we continue
down this this road of of policy blunder
after policy blunder out of the Trump
administration. And again, I say that as
a data driven centrist. Everyone knows
within our payw wall that I'm very much
not a supporter of either political
party here in the US. So, uh, you know,
I feel like I've earned the ethos
throughout my career across global Wall
Street to make these kinds of
statements. And quite frankly, someone
needs to make them. Most people who do
what I do for a living are scared to
make these kinds of statements because
they're going to get labeled as XYZ by
ABC group or ABC by XYZ group. And quite
frankly, uh I don't have any of that
kind of risk because you could check my
track record. It's very clear that we're
data driven centrist and everything that
comes out of my mouth is is from you
know really hardcore institutional
analysis and and nothing more. Uh so
getting back into the question um you
know in terms of our KISS portfolio
construction process for those who may
be new to me or new to 42 macro what
that is is our systematic risk
management overlay that we use to help
our retail clients manage their
portfolios especially uh in times of
high uncertainty. But in my opinion we
built the process to help retail
investors retire on time and comfortably
no matter what the economic scenario is.
Um, and so how that process works, it's
a 603010 trend following strategy that
features 60% stocks, uh, 30% gold and
and 10% Bitcoin. When the allocations
are maxed out, there are volatility
targeting and dynamic position sizing
elements, uh, which are two of the
hallmarks of institutional finance. And
from a risk management standpoint that
are infused into the process uh that
help it manage risk heading into bare
markets and ultimately get by uh, uh,
the dip uh, and reduce cost uh, cost
cost bases uh, after bare markets. So um
you know KISS has been kicking butt and
taking names since we uh introduced the
program and uh it continues to do so uh
here uh as well and so um you know
really proud of what we built. Uh but
just getting back into the question so
everyone has the context uh we use USFR
which are floating rate treasuries to
manage the cash allocation in KISS. I
tell our clients every week in our round
the horn presentations use whatever you
want to manage your own cash. Um you
know so we like floating rate treasuries
because it you know avoids all the uh uh
funkiness that we tend to see in the
bill market in and around these you know
increasingly high frequency uh debt
limit you know kind of um you know
brinksmanship type or that we've seen in
recent years. Uh but at the end of the
day a lot of our clients use money
market fund exposure uh they use uh
stable coins you know for those who are
you know in the crypto market uh etc. uh
but this question is really more about
the currency risk associated with that
and I would say if you're an
international investment investor
participating in KISS of which half of
our clients are international with
clients in 80 countries around the world
if you're an international investor
participating in KISS then use your own
home currency to do your cash exposure
you should not be making currency bets
to to to to park your cash I mean you
can you can do whatever you want and we
make it very easy we provide the tools
and the signals that the clients need to
customize KISS to however
uh uh would prefer from the from the
from the perspective of their risk
tolerance and their strategic investment
objectives. But at the end of the day,
what I think is most sound from an
investment standpoint is to when you
when you raise cash, you don't want to
be taking risk. That's the whole point
is of raising cash. And so uh you should
be denominating your cash allocations uh
in your home currency. Uh and so that
eliminates the currency risk. you know,
especially if you're a US investor and
you're concerned about a dollar
destabilizing, you know, what's going to
happen on the other side of a dollar
stabilizing is one, the gold allocations
and Bitcoin allocations in your KISS uh
uh portfolio are going to go up and and
for, you know, through the roof uh
through the moon to the moon and beyond
uh if you're looking at the chart of
gold and and eventually Bitcoin will
catch up uh in this or maybe even future
market cycles. Um, so that's that's
that's how you can hedge a destabilizing
dollar. If you're a US dollar uh based
investor, uh, so that that's the whole
purpose of that. And ultimately, if we
do see a destabilizing capital outflows
from the US economy, we know whose
balance sheet is going to be called upon
to to arrest that or to at least replace
those capital outflows with, you know,
printed money. And that's inter JP Pal
or whoever JP Pal gets replaced with at
the Fed in terms of implementing yield
curve control on the long end of the
Treasury curve. And if they do that,
then US stocks are going to go through
the roof as I mean that's exactly what
you see in countries like Argentina,
Venezuela, Turkey. You know these are
the Zimbabwe. This is the kind of stuff
that you tend to see in countries where
there's a lack of central bank
independence and the executive branch is
you know strongarmming the central bank
into doing exactly what it wants. Uh and
so in our opinion it's no surprise to
see stocks down, dollar stocks down a
lot, dollar down a lot, bonds down a lot
today with gold and bitcoin up a lot
today. This is exactly what we've been
preparing our clients for for years in
the context of our investing during a
fourth turning regime presentation. This
has always been the the most likely
outcome. You know, we're not we don't
have enough hubers to pretend like we
have we understand how this is all going
to play out with specificity. But what
we can do is prepare our clients for the
range of probable outcomes and
ultimately build a systematic trend
following portfolio that will help them
navigate the contours of this forth
turning poly crisis better than anyone
else in the world. We're really
confident in saying that because we
continue to prove that as we have proved
that over the past couple of years, two

been active. So, uh hopefully that
answers the question on on dollars.
Again, you know, you want to do in your

dollarbased investor concerned about a

and so uh what I'm more concerned about

erosion of US exorbitant privilege. It's
not the whether we lose it or not. It's
the erosion that matters to your
portfolio and it matters to financial
markets in terms of the path that
financial markets take to get there. And
so, uh, I think, you know, again, it's
not clear to me in my opinion, I think
that the Trump administration's policy

private sector demand you know that this
sort of bloated heavy-handed approach to
facilitating that transition is

bonds down dollar down this is the type

economies during a balance of payments
crisis let me say that again stocks down
bonds down dollar down is the the kind
of stuff you only see in emerging market
economies during a balance of payments
crisis there is a crisis of confidence
in US assets that is being facilitated
by Fisher price tariff formulas that are
now wrecking the global economy. Uh
policy blunder after policy blunder,
most notably of which or most recently
that include, you know, threatening
central bank independence at a during
the middle of what could potentially be
a balance of payments crisis. I couldn't
think of a dumber thing to do quite
frankly and I I I'm confident in saying
dumb because it is dumb. That just pull
up a financial market. If you're
listening to me, if you're in the Trump
administration listening to me talk
right now, and I know some of you I know
I know one of you very well. I know some
of you may be starting to tune in
because I can see who's following my
Twitter account now. It's really dumb to
threaten central bank independence
during a balance of payments crisis. And
we may very well be in the early innings
of a balance of payments crisis here in
the US as the crisis of confidence is
accelerating in the context of, you
know, just the rest of the world's
capital allocators not being so keen to
leave their assets parked in US assets.
We have a net international investment
deficit of $24 trillion. That's
twothirds of our GDP, more than
twothirds of our GDP. Again, no other
economy in the world in world history
has ever seen stuff like this. you know,
they could pull the plug on us really,
really quickly and really, really
aggressively if they're, you know, if
the political winds in their economies,
in the Japanese economy, Chinese
economy, the Euro zone economy shift in
a way that forces mutually assured
destruction on their behalf, this could
get very ugly uh uh uh not only in a
hurry, but could last for a really long
period of time in terms of the
persistent erosion of exorbitant
privilege and the capital the persistent
capital outflows that could potentially
uh uh perpetuate. So, uh, this is a big
risk. Uh, I'm not smart enough to tell
you how I how it's all going to end. I
don't think anybody's smart enough to
tell you exactly how it's all going to
end. So, anybody that tells you the US
is going to lose exorbitant privilege or
Trump's going to do this or that and,
you know, nobody knows. We don't know.
But what we can do is prepare for the
scenarios and ultimately build
systematic, you know, portfolios that
help investors navigate this without
having to use our puny human brains to
try to figure out where it's all going
to go because again, I don't think
anybody knows where it's all going to
go. And so going back to answering the
question, are we going to replace you
know a US equity beta with international
equity beta to that to me that's too big
of a question to ask and answer. uh you
know I went back and did some research
on this you know to to sort of just to
figure out okay you know right now what
we like about having US equity beta in
KISS is because a lot of the forth
turning poly crisis factors that will
you know sort of um you know catalyze
the central bank large s the the the
substantial monetary debasement of
financial repression that we're
ultimately expecting and have a high
probability uh uh um you know assessment
of uh uh with respect to the long term
that's going to be extremely beneficial
for US equities I mean again go look at
what happens in Venezuelan equities or
Zimbabwean equities or Argentinian
equities or Turkish equities when the
central bank has to step in and and and
essentially monetize uh uh deficits and
debts. So, I don't want to be on the
wrong side of that uh as a US-based
investor. But I am also keen to the risk
that hey, you could have sustained
relative outperformance of international
equities. There's hardly ever any, you
know, there's rarely a time series
history of US equities having
persistently negative absolute
performance and international equities
having persistent positive relative abs
or positive absolute performance. You've
seen long periods of time where
international equities outperformed US
equities. But again, KISS is not a
system that's designed to generate
alpha. Dr. Mo is the system that's
designed to generate alpha. And so
ultimately uh if your goal is to
generate alpha, which is what
institutional investors get paid to do,
then you should be focused on Dr. M. But
if your goal is to collect the beta that
the market gods supply us with so that
you can retire on time and comfortably,
these kinds of questions, in my opinion,
are are are not necessarily um uh uh uh
they're just you're you're sort of at
you're trying to solve a problem that
does not exist in the context of what I
think most retail investors should be
doing, which is just retire on time and
comfortably. Don't play captain hedge
fund manager and try to go well I think
US outperforms international and vice
versa. That's an institutional factor
long short question that you need that
those guys get paid tons of money to
answer. You're not getting paid at all
as a retail investor to answer that
question. So why even take that risk in
your portfolio especially in the context
of someone who is as prepared for this
this fourth turning product crisis as
anybody in the world outside of maybe
Ray Dallio then I don't even know the
answer. So that that's kind of my my
general take on that. But the one thing
I'll say just wrapping up on this
question is there's hardly ever in the
history of modern financial markets a a
sustained period of time where US
equities were going down for a long
period of time and international
equities are going up for a long period
of time. And so at the end of the day,
what's probably going to happen if we
had a significant sustained bare market
in US equities as a function of capital
outflows, you're going to have US
economic the economy go down in terms of
uh its productivity and its overall uh
growth rate. That's going to drag the
rest of the world down and global
earnings are going to just be
persistently lower than current, you
know, structural expectations. And
you're going to have all equity markets
going down in that scenario. and and and
in a in a systematic system like KISS
which again doesn't factor in any of my
fundamental research views of which you
know I think they're as well researched
as many investors uh in the world it
doesn't factor in any of my fundamental
research views what that systematic
signal will what the systematic kiss
process will signal to our clients is
that equities are bearish from the
perspective of our VAM signal and the
broader market regime is in a riskoff
market so you won't have any equity
allocation in your kiss portfolio uh uh
in that in that type in in scenario
where it would actually make sense to
consider uh rotating out of US equities
because again you're probably going to
still see negative absolute returns of
international equities over a long
enough time horizon. So uh hopefully I
